text: The surgeon points out the inferior epigastric vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the procedure at the medial umbilical ligament and progresses laterally, likely in preparation for addressing the indirect inguinal hernia on the right side.
The surgeon attempts to enter the preperitoneal plane, encountering a thin and tightly fused layer between the two tissue planes.
The surgeon moves laterally, accepting the fused layers of the preperitoneal plane as they continue the dissection.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz on top of the fat layer, visible as a yellowish tissue, while progressing laterally in the surgical field.
The surgeon pushes the fat laterally, positioning it above the visible nerves in the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to re-establish the preperitoneal plane from the undersurface of the flap, but the tissue is too thin to effectively separate the layers.
The surgeon works medially in the supravesicular space, navigating the thin preperitoneal plane.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz on top of the adipose tissue, maintaining a lateral approach to avoid underlying structures.
The surgeon continues dissection down to the infra-upper gastric region, focusing on the anatomical area beneath the stomach.
The surgeon switches to an oblique angle and uses electrocautery to buzz directly on the hernia sac, visibly pushing the surrounding fat away from the surgical site.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the end of the hernia sac, revealing the vas deferens as it comes into view.
The surgeon uses cautery to gently touch the tissue, applying minimal energy to the area surrounding the hernia sac.
The surgeon maintains the position superior and medial to the fat, using cautery to manipulate the tissue around the hernia sac.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz directly on top of the hernia sac, visibly manipulating the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon uses an oblique angle to reveal the vas deferens, which becomes visible as it emerges from the surrounding tissue near the hernia sac.
The surgeon demonstrates the preserved deferential vessel running medial to the vas, maintaining its integrity during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the cautery device to apply a few pulses, causing the tissue to fall away as they pull up medially around the corner.
The surgeon performs gentle sweeping motions with an instrument, likely to manipulate or separate tissue in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully picks up the sac, which is visible creeping around the corner, while approaching closely to the deferential vessel running medially on the vas.
The surgeon displays the spermatic cord, which is visibly well preserved, maintaining its structural integrity.
The surgical field shows the absence of a cord lipoma, indicating a clear view of the spermatic cord without any fatty tissue masses.
The surgeon identifies the iliac vessels, focusing on the right side, using careful dissection techniques to expose these structures.
The surgeon performs a medial dissection near the iliac vein, demonstrating increased swiftness and boldness in the technique.
The surgeon performs a sweeping motion to further dissect and separate tissue layers, maintaining proximity to the deferential vessel.
The surgeon exposes the iliopubic tract, noting the presence of a small hernia sac in the surgical field.
The surgical field shows no evidence of a cord lipoma during the examination.
The surgeon performs high lateral fixation using two ovicral sutures, securing the tissue in place.
The surgeon performs Cooper's ligament fixation using two ovicral sutures, securing the anatomical structures in place.
The surgeon observes the clear undersurface of the muscle, revealing its straight alignment and texture.
The surgeon performs a typical flap closure, bringing together the edges of the flap to cover the surgical site.
